2604.27031.title: NORACL: Neurogenesis for Oracle-free Resource-Adaptive Continual Learning
2604.27031.paper_summary: In a continual learning setting, we require a model to be plastic enough to learn a new task and stable enough to not disturb previously learned capabilities. We argue that this dilemma has an architectural root. A finite network has limited representational and plastic resources, yet the required capacity depends on properties of the future task stream that are unknown: how many tasks will be encountered, and how much they overlap in feature space. Regularization-based methods preserve past knowledge within fixed-capacity architectures and therefore implicitly rely on an oracle architecture sized for this unknown future. When tasks are only weakly related, fixed architectures progressively run out of plastic resources; when tasks are few or strongly overlapping, models are often over-provisioned. Inspired by neurogenesis in biology, we propose NORACL to address the stability-plasticity dilemma by tackling the oracle architecture problem through neuronal growth. Starting from a compact network, NORACL grows only when needed by monitoring two complementary signals for representational and plasticity saturation. We evaluate NORACL against oracle-sized static baselines across varying task counts and geometries. Across all settings, NORACL achieves final average accuracies that are better than or on par with oracle-provisioned static baselines while using fewer parameters. Additionally, NORACL yields architectures with interpretable growth, i.e. dissimilar tasks predominantly expand feature-extraction layers, whereas tasks which rely on common features shift growth toward later feature-combination layers. Our analysis further explains why fixed-capacity networks lose plasticity as tasks accumulate, whereas NORACL creates fresh capacity for new tasks through growth. Together, these results show that adaptive neurogenesis pushes the stability-plasticity Pareto frontier of continual learning.
2604.27031.paper_summary_zh: 在持續學習的環境中，我們需要一個模型，既要足夠靈活以學習新任務，又要足夠穩定以不干擾先前學習的能力。<lb/>我們認為這一困境有其架構上的根源。<lb/>有限的網絡擁有有限的表徵和可塑資源，而所需的容量取決於未來任務流的特性，這些特性是未知的：將會遇到多少任務，以及它們在特徵空間中的重疊程度。<lb/>基於正則化的方法在固定容量的架構中保留過去的知識，因此隱含地依賴於為這一未知未來設計的神諭架構。<lb/>當任務之間的關聯性較弱時，固定架構逐漸耗盡可塑資源；當任務較少或強烈重疊時，模型往往會過度配置。<lb/>受到生物學中神經生成的啟發，我們提出了NORACL，以通過神經元增長來解決穩定性-可塑性困境，並處理神諭架構問題。<lb/>從一個緊湊的網絡開始，NORACL僅在需要時增長，通過監控兩個互補信號來檢測表徵和可塑性飽和。<lb/>我們將NORACL與在不同任務數量和幾何形狀下的神諭大小靜態基準進行評估。<lb/>在所有設置中，NORACL達到了比神諭配置的靜態基準更好或相當的最終平均準確率，同時使用的參數更少。<lb/>此外，NORACL產生的架構具有可解釋的增長，即不相似的任務主要擴展特徵提取層，而依賴共同特徵的任務則將增長轉向後期特徵組合層。<lb/>我們的分析進一步解釋了為什麼固定容量的網絡在任務累積時會失去可塑性，而NORACL則通過增長為新任務創造新的容量。<lb/>綜合這些結果顯示，自適應神經生成推進了持續學習的穩定性-可塑性帕累托邊界。
2604.27031.author: Karthik Charan Raghunathan et.al.
2604.27031.authors: Karthik Charan Raghunathan, Christian Metzner, Laura Kriener, Melika Payvand
